misconceptionSummary: Students double a number when asked to square it, or triple it when asked to cube it.

lines.text: Multiplied by the exponent — exponent means factor count

steps.narration: The student treated the exponent two as a multiplier, calculating six times two. But the exponent tells you how many times to use six as a factor — not what to multiply six by. Six squared means six times six.

visuals.ruleText: n² means n appears as a factor 2 times: n × n
visuals.subText: n³ means n appears as a factor 3 times: n × n × n

steps.narration: The exponent counts how many copies of the base number are multiplied together. Two means two copies of the base: six times six. Three means three copies: six times six times six. The exponent is a count of factors, not a multiplier.

Six squared is six times six, which is thirty-six — not twelve. Six cubed is six times six times six: thirty-six times six is two hundred and sixteen — not eighteen. The difference between multiplying by the exponent and using it as a factor count is enormous.

visuals.label: Exponent = factor count, not multiplier: 6² = 6×6, not 6×2

steps.narration: Thirty-six and two hundred and sixteen. The exponent is a count of factors — write out that many copies of the base and multiply them together.

misconceptionStrip.text: Common mistake: 6² = 12 (multiplied by 2 instead of squaring). Six squared means 6 × 6 = 36. The exponent 2 counts factors — it does not multiply.
misconceptionStrip.audioNarration: The exponent is not a multiplier. It tells you how many times to write the base number as a factor. Six squared means six times six equals thirty-six — not six times two equals twelve.
